narrative_blocks.body: Joshua Talcott died in Bolton on March 22, 1799, aged 87. His wife Rachel Hollister outlived him by eight years, dying in Bolton in 1807 at age 94. His son Josiah was killed at age 9 by the fall of a tree (1766); his son Job married Abigail Huntington and fathered Gurdon, whose line extended into the Granite Mills of Talcottville.

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Hollister and Dorothy Hill of Glastonbury

death.cause: killed by fall of a tree

narrative_blocks.header: Killed by a falling tree at age 8
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Talcott was born April 30, 1757 and killed by the fall of a tree on March 10, 1766 — age 8. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.
Samuel died in 1770, aged about seven.

political_offices.title: Deputy to General Court
political_offices.notes: Secretary of the General Court in absence of Col. Allyn during October session 1684

political_offices.title: Member, Standing Committee
political_offices.notes: Appointed to order measures in intervals of General Court

political_offices.title: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County
Assistant
political_offices.notes: From 1683 except during Andros' administration, was Assistant until his death

will.summary: Will of Captain Samuel Talcott, Wethersfield CT. Bequeathed to sons: Samuel (home lot south, great meadow parcels, Westfield lot); John (home lot north, 5 acres great meadow, 12 acres Westfield); Elizur (Westfield brook land, 6 acres meadow, 50-acre lot near Farmington line); Joseph (30 acres Westfield); Benjamin (north half, east side Connecticut River, undivided Indian lands, south half 200-acre court grant); Nathaniel (south half Nawburk lot, undivided east-river land, north half 200-acre grant, one cow). Daughters: Hannah Chester £90; Rachel £80. Wife Mary: £10/yr, use of lower room, garden, one cow during widowhood. Executor: son Samuel Talcott. Overseers: Capt. Robert Wells, Ensign Samuel Butler.
will.note: Original will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.97-102.

T00036.notable_legacy: "From these two sons, John and Samuel, are descended all of the name of TALCOTT in America." — Talcott Pedigree (1876). All living Talcotts in America descend from Capt. Samuel and/or his brother John (T00035).

Capt. Samuel Talcott's wife Hannah Holyoke was the daughter of.

narrative_blocks.body: Harvard College 1658 — the scholar of the Talcott family; his father bequeathed all his books to Samuel (except the 'Martyr Book' given to John)

narrative_blocks.header: One of the original proprietors of the town
narrative_blocks.body: One of the original proprietors of the town of Glastonbury, CT — owned the lot purchased by his father John in 1643 of Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve, 44 rods wide running three miles to the wilderness; still owned by descendants at time of writing

narrative_blocks.body: Direct ancestor of the compiler Samuel Talcott Hooker through the maternal Talcott line

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Hartford. Name inscribed on monument erected by citizens of Hartford to perpetuate memory of the founders of the Colony of Connecticut.

political_offices.title: Select Man (Selectman)
political_offices.notes: Chosen at general town meeting Feb 4, 1634 along with Haynes, Bradstreet, and four others

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: One of Representatives for Newtown (Cambridge), May 14, 1634

emigration.notes: 123 passengers including 50 children; all arrived in good health after 12-week passage. Fellow passengers included William Wadsworth, Tobie Willet (possible relation to Mary Willet who married Rev. Samuel Hooker), James Olmstedd, and others of Hooker's Company.

hartford_land.notes: Nine parcels originally granted: dwelling house on east side of Main Street abutting Centinel Hill; seven acres east of Great River; five acres in North Meadow; forty-seven acres meadow/swamp in North Meadow; one acre in Little Meadow; thirty acres in Old Oxpasture; seventeen acres in Cow Pasture; eleven acres in Venturers Field; sixteen acres in Neck of Land. Also gained 25 additional parcels by acquisition.

will.bequests_summary: Dwelling house and lands to wife Dorothy for her natural life; then to son John. Wethersfield property to son Samuel. Grandchildren John Russell (£20), John Talcott, Samuel Talcott, Elizabeth Talcott (£5 each). £5 to Rev. Samuel Stone; £5 toward a Latin school at Hartford. Overseers: Richard Lord Sr. and son John.
will.gift_to_stone: Five pounds to 'my reverend and beloved teacher, Mr. Stone' — Rev. Samuel Stone, Hartford minister and colleague of Thomas Hooker

cross_connections.display_label: Tobie Willet was a fellow passenger on the Lion in.

was his grandson, the long-serving Barnstable minister

narrative_blocks.header: The only Talcott known to have emigrated
narrative_blocks.body: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America — all American Talcotts descend from him
Sailed on ship Lyon June 22, 1632 with Hooker's Company, arriving Boston Sept 16, 1632

narrative_blocks.header: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who
narrative_blocks.body: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who explored the Hartford area October 1635, prior to Hooker's party departure May 1636

narrative_blocks.header: Fifth greatest proprietor of houses and lands
narrative_blocks.body: Fifth greatest proprietor of houses and lands in Newtown (Cambridge) 1634

narrative_blocks.header: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house
narrative_blocks.body: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house in Hartford on the ground where the North Church (Dr. Bushnell's) later stood

narrative_blocks.header: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott'
narrative_blocks.body: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott' in colonial records

narrative_blocks.header: Mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John S…
narrative_blocks.body: His mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John Steele; her sister Sarah married Hartford Founder William Wadsworth; he was an uncle of Hartford Founder John Skinner

bio.notes: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America. Co-founder of Hartford with Rev. Thomas Hooker.

parents.discrepancy_note: One source gives parents as 'John Mott and Alice Harington'; Talcott 1876 gives 'Mark Mott and Frances Gutter' with Mark being son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott. The Talcott 1876 reconstruction preferred as more detailed.
parents.mother_research_notes: Probably Frances Gutter (wife of Mark Mott). Frances died Braintree, buried Feb 23, 1615. Mark Mott died Braintree, buried Dec 14, 1637.
parents.father_research_notes: Probably Mark Mott, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott of Braintree, Essex. Talcott 1876 says 'supposed to have been the daughter of Mark, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott.'

parents.mother_research_notes: Prudence, daughter of Rev. John Stockton of Kinholdt, England; married Edward Holyoke June 18, 1612

narrative_blocks.header: He married Springfield’s founders, then linked Hartford’s
narrative_blocks.body: Crossing from Tamworth, England, Elizur Holyoke married Mary Pynchon, daughter of Springfield's founder William Pynchon, and rose to prominent office in the Massachusetts colony. His daughter Hannah's marriage to Captain Samuel Talcott then joined the founding families of Springfield and Hartford.

parents.notes: Daughter of Hon. William Pynchon of Springfield, MA

burial.plot_notes: Springfield MA — gravestone still standing 1876 per Talcott Pedigree.
